pages.full_text:
the engine was away being repaired. A knowledgable guide showed us the wild flowers – purple violets & [?Sor] Scurvy grass that are lead tolerant, + grow above the lead seams. Hare's tales is another of them. There are 4 in the area. The seams of lead lie vertically. It was first mined by the Romans. When miners suspect lead is below the surface, they do hushing.

        The Bowers
        bloom May-Aug
        The miners
        divert streams [?for]
        [?ix] for a 6 mile[s]
        + dam them +
        then explode the
        dam + drive
        away peat +
photo_captions: Killhope's Armstrong Water Wheel / Killhope Lead Mining Museum, / Upper Weardale, Co Durham.
pages.notes: Page continues from a previous spread; text at top begins mid-sentence. The caption for the photo is written vertically/rotated alongside the photograph of the mining waterwheel structure. The small inset text beside the photo describes hushing technique (diverting streams). 'ix' or similar numeral before 'for a 6 mile' is unclear — could be a correction or annotation. 'Hare's tales' likely refers to Hare's-tail cottongrass (Eriophorum vaginatum), a lead-tolerant plant.

pages.full_text:
shale to reveal the lead seams. Ponies brought the lead in the form of galena out of the mines in heavy tubs on rails. Boys washed the galena. Floride is found as well.

        Working conditions & living conditions were hard – 32 men slept in one room. They died in their 40s of "black spit."

        The mine owner provided schools. literacy was 70% whereas in coal mines it was 3%. These miners were so well thought-of that mine owners in Australia would pay their passage there.

        A Gospel Ship – the Methodists were strong here. We looked at the shops in Alston & then came on to a campsite near St. John's Chapel.
pages.notes: Page number '50' visible in top-right corner. A glued-in colour illustration of 'The Gospel Ship' (a diagrammatic sailing ship with text on its sails, a Victorian/Edwardian evangelical tract image) occupies most of the right side of the page. 'Floride' is as written — likely means Fluorite/Fluorspar. The narrative describes Killhope Lead Mining Museum (Upper Weardale) and ends with travel to a campsite near St. John's Chapel. No year is explicit on this page; context (volume B, near end of volume, Upper Weardale/Killhope area) suggests a trip likely in the 2010s but year cannot be confirmed from page content alone.
